config.title: Berlin Wall Trail: FAQ
config.subtitle: How long the trail really is, which section to pick, what survives, and what the surface and tickets are actually like.

items.q: How long is the Berlin Wall Trail?
items.a: 160 km. It follows the border around the whole of former West Berlin, so it is a loop rather than a line. About 50 km runs through the city and the remaining 110 km or so passes through forest, lakes, fields and outer suburbs in Berlin and Brandenburg.

items.q: How much of the Berlin Wall can you actually still see along the trail?
items.a: Much less than most people expect. The East Side Gallery is 1,316 metres, the longest surviving stretch anywhere. The Topography of Terror has about 200 metres of original Wall. Bernauer Strasse preserves roughly 70 metres of the full-depth border system, which is the only place in Berlin where the complete arrangement of outer wall, death strip, patrol road, signal fence and hinterland wall still stands together. Beyond those, you get scattered fragments, victim steles and a handful of surviving watchtowers.

items.q: Which section of the Berlin Wall Trail should I walk if I only have an afternoon?
items.a: Nordbahnhof to Mauerpark. It is about 2 km and it carries the highest density of Wall history on the whole 160 km: the 1.4 km Bernauer Strasse memorial, the preserved full-depth border strip, the Chapel of Reconciliation, a surviving watchtower, and then Mauerpark at the far end. Everything there is free, and the outdoor memorial grounds are open daily from 08:00 to 22:00, so it works early or late when other sites are shut.

items.q: Can you cycle the Berlin Wall Trail on a normal rental bike?
items.a: On the city section, yes. On the outer loop, think twice. The Senate’s own route flyers state that the Mauerweg is not a developed cycle path and that the surface alternates between asphalt, forest floor, cobbles and field track, with short diversions where the path is damaged and occasional stretches where you have to get off and push. A basic share bike is fine from Nordbahnhof through the centre to Warschauer Strasse. For the Spandau forest, the Gatow fields or the southern farmland, a trekking bike with wider tyres is the realistic choice.

items.q: Do I need a separate ticket for my bike on Berlin public transport?
items.a: Yes, and the Deutschlandticket does not include it. Every bike carried needs its own bicycle ticket within the VBB network, on top of your own valid ticket. Bikes are allowed on the U-Bahn, S-Bahn, tram, ferry and night bus lines N1 to N9, but not on normal daytime buses. Board only at the doors marked with a bike symbol, expect a maximum of two bikes per marked area in each carriage, and remember that prams and wheelchairs have priority, so staff can ask you to get off. A fully folded folding bike counts as hand luggage and travels free.

items.q: Where are the surviving watchtowers on the Berlin Wall Trail?
items.a: Four are worth knowing. The Schlesischer Busch command post on Puschkinallee in Treptow is a four-storey concrete tower that once controlled 18 watchtowers, with a monument-listed slab wall beside it. The Guenter Litfin memorial tower at Kieler Strasse 2 is free but opens only at weekends from 11:00 to 17:00 between 1 May and 5 October, with a limit of 15 people and no children under 8. The Nieder Neuendorf tower near Hennigsdorf is a free four-level museum open Tuesday to Sunday, 10:00 to 18:00, reached by bus 136 rather than by train. A fourth tower stands inside the Bernauer Strasse memorial grounds.

items.q: Is the Berlin Wall Trail well signposted?
items.a: Mostly, but it is in the middle of an upgrade. The route is marked with signs reading Berliner Mauerweg in white on grey, and the official flyers say the waymarking and overview boards are enough to follow. Since 2023 a newer Corten steel system of information steles and markers has been rolling out, starting with the 1.1 km Flutgraben stretch at Schlesischer Busch, with around 130 more steles planned across the full route. That means signage quality varies noticeably by section. Downloading the official GPS tracks before you go is the safer move.

items.q: Is the whole Berlin Wall Trail inside Berlin?
items.a: No, and that costs you money if you forget. The route crosses into Brandenburg at Hennigsdorf, Hohen Neuendorf, Stolpe, Teltow, Kleinmachnow, Stahnsdorf, Griebnitzsee and Potsdam. Those all sit outside fare zone B, so you need an ABC ticket, and since the BC zone was abolished on 1 January 2026 there is no cheaper middle option any more. The bike ticket has to match the same zones.

items.q: Is any part of the Berlin Wall Trail closed in 2026?
items.a: A few stretches. The Hahneberg section in Spandau is still shut while the rebuild is finished, with completion expected during 2026. The shore path at Gross Glienicker See is currently interrupted. The lakeside path at Griebnitzsee is partly blocked by residents in a long-running legal dispute, and the city of Potsdam routes walkers via Rudolf-Breitscheid-Strasse, Karl-Marx-Strasse and Virchowstrasse instead. Separately, the Stadtbahn closure from 14 June to 12 December 2026 disrupts regional trains including the RE1 towards Potsdam, though the S-Bahn runs normally, so use the S7 or S1 for Griebnitzsee.

items.q: Where does the Berlin Wall Trail start?
items.a: Anywhere you like. It is a closed loop, it is signed in both directions, and the official flyers say explicitly that you can join it at any point. The traditional starting point is the Brandenburg Gate going clockwise, but the only real mistake is starting somewhere on the outer loop and never reaching the city section, because that is where the Wall you came to see actually is.
